questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: What is the pH of a 0.010 M solution of a strong acid $\text{HCl}$?

choices.explanation: pH = 1 corresponds to $[\text{H}^+] = 0.10$ M (0.10 M HCl), not 0.010 M.
$\text{HCl}$ is a strong acid that fully dissociates; $[\text{H}^+] = 0.010$ M $= 10^{-2}$ M; $\text{pH} = -\log(10^{-2}) = 2$.
pH = 10 indicates a basic solution; 0.010 M HCl is acidic (pH < 7).
pH = 12 corresponds to $[\text{OH}^-] = 0.010$ M, which would be a 0.010 M strong base solution, not an acid.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is the conjugate base of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$?

choices.explanation: $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ is the conjugate base of $\text{HSO}_4^-$, not $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$; each proton transfer changes the species by one $\text{H}^+$.
$\text{H}_3\text{SO}_4^+$ would be the conjugate acid (gains a proton); conjugate base loses a proton.
The conjugate base is formed when an acid donates one proton; $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 - \text{H}^+ = \text{HSO}_4^-$.
$\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is the conjugate base of $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$, not $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A weak acid $\text{HA}$ has $K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$. What is the $K_b$ of its conjugate base $\text{A}^-$?

choices.explanation: $K_b$ of the conjugate base is NOT equal to $K_a$; they are related by $K_a \times K_b = K_w$.
$1.8 \times 10^9$ would be $K_a / K_w$; the correct formula is $K_b = K_w / K_a$.
$1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ is $K_w$, the product of $K_a$ and $K_b$, not $K_b$ itself.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A buffer contains 0.40 mol $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ (acetic acid, $\text{p}K_a = 4.74$) and 0.20 mol $\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}$ in 1.00 L. What is the pH?

choices.explanation: 5.04 = 4.74 + 0.30, which would result from $\log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]) = +0.30$, meaning $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}] = 2$; here the ratio is 0.5 (inverted).
4.74 = pKa, which is the pH only when $[\text{A}^-] = [\text{HA}]$ (at the half-equivalence point); here $[\text{A}^-] < [\text{HA}]$, so pH < pKa.
3.74 would require $\log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]) = -1.0$, meaning a 10:1 ratio of acid to base; the actual ratio is 0.40:0.20 = 2:1.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: At the half-equivalence point of a weak acid–strong base titration, which statement is true?

choices.explanation: At the half-equivalence point, exactly half the weak acid has been neutralized; $[\text{HA}] = [\text{A}^-]$, so $\log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]) = 0$ and $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$.

choices.text: All acid has been neutralized and pH = 7
choices.explanation: Complete neutralization occurs at the equivalence point, not half-equivalence; pH = 7 only for strong acid-strong base at equivalence.

choices.text: pH equals 7 because the solution is neutral
choices.explanation: pH = 7 occurs at the equivalence point of a strong acid–strong base titration; at the half-equivalence point, pH = pKa (which is less than 7 for weak acids).
pH = 2pKa has no thermodynamic basis; at the half-equivalence point, Henderson-Hasselbalch gives pH = pKa (when $[A^-] = [HA]$).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following pairs makes the most effective buffer near pH 4.74? ($K_a$ of acetic acid = $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$, $K_a$ of benzoic acid = $6.5 \times 10^{-5}$, $K_a$ of formic acid = $1.8 \ times 10^{-4}$)

choices.explanation: Benzoic acid has $\text{p}K_a = -\log(6.5 \times 10^{-5}) \approx 4.19$; most effective buffer range is $\text{p}K_a \pm 1 = 3.19-5.19$, which includes 4.74 but is farther from the target pH.
Acetic acid has $\text{p}K_a = -\log(1.8 \times 10^{-5}) = 4.74$; a buffer is most effective when pH = pKa, making acetic acid/acetate the ideal choice for pH 4.74.
Formic acid has $\text{p}K_a = -\log(1.8 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 3.74$; buffering near pH 4.74 (1 unit above pKa) would be less effective.
HCl is a strong acid and NaCl is its salt; strong acid–salt pairs do not form effective buffers because HCl fully dissociates.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: What is the pH of a solution with $[\text{OH}^-] = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ M at 25°C?

choices.explanation: pH 7.00 is neutral; $[\text{OH}^-] = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ M is basic (greater than $10^{-7}$ M), so pH > 7.
This would correspond to $[\text{OH}^-] = 10^{-5.40}$ M; the calculation gives $\text{pH} = 14 - 2.60 = 11.40$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Calculate the pH of a 0.100 M solution of acetic acid ($K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$).

choices.explanation: pH = 5.00 would mean $[\text{H}^+] = 10^{-5}$ M; the calculation for 0.100 M acetic acid gives $[\text{H}^+] = \sqrt{K_a \times C} = \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.34 \times 10^{-3}$ M, pH = 2.87.
$[\text{H}^+] \approx \sqrt{K_a \times C} = \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.100} = \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.34 \times 10^{-3}$ M; $\text{pH} = -\log(1.34 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.87$.
pH 1.00 corresponds to $[\text{H}^+] = 0.100$ M, which would be the case if acetic acid were a strong acid; it is weak and only partially dissociates.
pH = 4.74 = pKa of acetic acid; this equals the pH of the buffer when $[\text{HA}] = [\text{A}^-]$, not the pH of a pure 0.100 M acetic acid solution.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is the strongest acid?

choices.explanation: $K_a = 6.8 \times 10^{-4}$ makes HF the weakest of the options listed (though it is the strongest hydrohalic weak acid).
Acetic acid has a smaller $K_a$ than HF, indicating weaker acid strength.
$K_a = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}$ is larger than acetic acid but smaller than HF; HF is still stronger.
$\text{HCl}$ is a strong acid that essentially completely dissociates; its $K_a \gg 10^6$, far exceeding all the weak acids listed.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: At the equivalence point of a titration between 50.0 mL of 0.100 M $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ and 0.100 M $\text{NaOH}$, what salt is present in solution and what is the approximate pH?

choices.explanation: $\text{NaCH}_3\text{COO}$ is present, but pH ≠ 7.00; the acetate ion hydrolyzes ($\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{OH}^-$), making the solution basic (pH > 7).
At equivalence, only $\text{NaCH}_3\text{COO}$ remains. $\text{Na}^+$ is spectator; $\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-$ is a weak base that hydrolyzes to form $\text{OH}^-$, raising pH above 7.
NaCl is the salt of a strong acid (HCl) – strong base titration; here HCl is replaced with acetic acid (weak acid), so NaCH₃COO forms, not NaCl.
Sodium acetate is a salt of a weak acid and strong base; it forms a basic solution (pH > 7), not acidic.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is an amphoteric species?

choices.explanation: $\text{HCl}$ is a strong acid only; it always donates a proton and cannot accept one in aqueous solution.
$\text{NaOH}$ is a strong base only; $\text{Na}^+$ is spectator and $\text{OH}^-$ only accepts protons.

Water is amphoteric — it can donate a proton ($\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{OH}^- + \text{H}^+$, acting as acid) or accept one ($\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{H}_3\text{O}^+$, acting as base).
$\text{NaCl}$ dissociates into $\text{Na}^+$ (spectator) and $\text{Cl}^-$ (very weak base, conjugate of strong acid HCl); neither is amphoteric.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Why does $\text{HI}$ have a lower $\text{p}K_a$ (stronger acid) than $\text{HF}$ despite fluorine being more electronegative than iodine?

choices.text: Electronegativity is the only factor determining acid strength
choices.explanation: Both electronegativity AND bond strength (bond length) determine acid strength; the H–I bond is weaker and longer than H–F.

choices.text: The H–I bond is much weaker than H–F, making it easier to break and release H⁺
choices.explanation: H–F bond dissociation energy (~565 kJ/mol) is much larger than H–I (~297 kJ/mol); the weaker, longer H–I bond breaks more easily, making HI a stronger acid despite iodine's lower electronegativity.

choices.text: Iodine is a better electron donor than fluorine
choices.explanation: Iodine has lower electronegativity than fluorine; it doesn't pull electron density away from H as effectively. The key factor is bond strength, not electron donation.

choices.text: HI forms a stronger hydrogen bond with water than HF
choices.explanation: HF forms stronger hydrogen bonds with water than HI; this actually contributes to HF being a weaker acid (stronger bond to water = harder to dissociate).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A buffer is made by dissolving 0.50 mol $\text{NH}_3$ ($K_b = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$; $\text{p}K_a$ of $\text{NH}_4^+$ = 9.26) and 0.50 mol $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ in 1.00 L. What is the pH?

choices.explanation: 4.74 is the pKa of acetic acid; for the ammonium-ammonia buffer, pH = pKa of NH₄⁺ = 9.26 when $[\text{NH}_3] = [\text{NH}_4^+]$.
$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]) = 9.26 + \log(0.50/0.50) = 9.26 + 0 = 9.26$; equal concentrations gives pH = pKa.
4.74 is pKa of acetic acid; the ammonium buffer uses pKa = 9.26 (pKa of NH₄⁺).
pH 7.00 is neutral water; the NH₃/NH₄⁺ buffer has pH = pKa of NH₄⁺ = 9.26 at equal concentrations.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For a diprotic acid $\text{H}_2\text{A}$, $K_{a1} = 1.2 \times 10^{-2}$ and $K_{a2} = 6.2 \times 10^{-8}$. Which statement is correct?

choices.text: Both protons are equally easy to remove
choices.explanation: $K_{a1} \gg K_{a2}$; the first proton (closest to a neutral molecule) is ~$10^6$ times easier to remove than the second (from a negatively charged ion).

choices.text: $K_{a2} < K_{a1}$ because removing the second proton is harder (negative charge repels proton donation)
choices.explanation: After losing the first proton, the resulting $\text{HA}^-$ carries a negative charge that stabilizes it and makes it harder to donate the second proton; $K_{a2}$ is always less than $K_{a1}$ for polyprotic acids.

choices.text: The second dissociation determines the total $[\text{H}^+]$ in solution
choices.explanation: The first dissociation ($K_{a1}$) dominates $[\text{H}^+]$ because it is much larger; the second dissociation contributes negligibly to $[\text{H}^+]$.
$K_a \times K_b = K_w$ applies to a conjugate acid-base pair; $K_{a1}$ and $K_{a2}$ are successive dissociation constants of the same acid and have no such relationship.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is a Lewis acid-base reaction?

choices.explanation: While this is an acid-base reaction (Brønsted-Lowry), it involves proton transfer; the Lewis definition emphasizes electron pair donation/acceptance.
$\text{BF}_3$ is an electron pair acceptor (Lewis acid — empty $p$ orbital on B); $\text{NH}_3$ is an electron pair donor (Lewis base — lone pair on N); no proton transfer occurs.
This is just NaOH dissolving in water (an Arrhenius process); no electron pair donation/acceptance between molecular species occurs.
This is a decomposition reaction, not an acid-base reaction of any type.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly lists the strong acids? (select the complete set)

choices.explanation: These six (plus HF is NOT in the list — HF is weak) are the six common strong acids that completely dissociate in water. Some lists add HClO₃.
$\text{HF}$ is a weak acid ($K_a = 6.8 \times 10^{-4}$) and does NOT belong in the strong acid list despite fluorine's high electronegativity.
Acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$) is a weak acid with $K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$; it partially dissociates and does not belong with strong acids.
Among the hydrohalic acids, only HCl, HBr, and HI are strong acids; HF is weak. This list incorrectly includes HF and omits HNO₃, H₂SO₄, and HClO₄.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Based on the titration data above, what is the $K_a$ of the weak acid being titrated?

choices.explanation: At 12.5 mL NaOH (half-equivalence point), pH = pKa = 4.74; $K_a = 10^{-4.74} = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$. This is the half-equivalence point where $[\text{HA}] = [\text{A}^-]$.
$K_a = 10^{-8.72}$ would be read from the equivalence point pH, which is not the pKa; pKa = pH at the half-equivalence point (12.5 mL).
$K_a = 10^{-7}$ corresponds to pH = 7; the pKa is read from the half-equivalence point pH = 4.74.
$K_a = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$ corresponds to pH = 2.30, not 4.74 (the half-equivalence pH).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies an Arrhenius base?

choices.explanation: $\text{NH}_3$ is a Brønsted-Lowry base (accepts H⁺) and Lewis base (donates lone pair); it is NOT an Arrhenius base because it does not produce $\text{OH}^-$ directly.
The Arrhenius definition: a base produces $\text{OH}^-$ ions in water. $\text{NaOH(aq)} \rightarrow \text{Na}^+\text{(aq)} + \text{OH}^-\text{(aq)}$.
$\text{BF}_3$ is a Lewis ACID (accepts electrons); it is not an Arrhenius base.
$\text{HCl}$ is an Arrhenius acid (produces $\text{H}^+$), not a base.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following oxoacids is the strongest acid?

choices.explanation: $\text{HClO}$ has the fewest oxygen atoms; fewer oxygens means less electron withdrawal from the O–H bond, making it the weakest of the chlorine oxoacids.
$\text{HClO}_2$ has 2 oxygen atoms; more oxygens than $\text{HClO}$ but fewer than $\text{HClO}_3$ and $\text{HClO}_4$, so it is intermediate in strength.
$\text{HClO}_3$ has 3 oxygen atoms and is a strong acid, but $\text{HClO}_4$ with 4 oxygens is even stronger.

For oxoacids with the same central atom, strength increases with more oxygen atoms (more O withdraws electron density from O–H, making H⁺ easier to release). $\text{HClO}_4$ is the strongest of the four chlorine oxoacids and one of the strongest known acids.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A solution has pH = 3.50. What is the $[\text{H}_3\text{O}^+]$ concentration?

choices.explanation: $1.00 \times 10^{-7}$ M corresponds to pH = 7 (neutral water), not pH 3.50.
